2026-07-07.headline: Pahalgam Attack: NIA Files Supplementary Chargesheet Against LeT Chief
2026-07-07.summary: The NIA filed a supplementary chargesheet before the NIA Special Court in Jammu against Hafiz Saeed — chief of Pakistan-based Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) and its proxy The Resistance Front (TRF) — in the Pahalgam terror attack that killed 26 civilians on 22 April 2025. The accused are charged under BNS 2023 and UAPA 1967, including 'waging war against India'.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Cross-border terrorism, anti-terror law (UAPA/BNS) and India-Pakistan security are high-frequency GS 3 and GS 2 themes. Charging terror outfits as legal entities is a notable prosecutorial strategy.

deepDive.explanation: The National Investigation Agency (NIA) filed a supplementary chargesheet in the Pahalgam terror attack case — the 22 April 2025 attack in which 26 civilians were killed. It named Hafiz Saeed, founder and chief of Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) and its proxy The Resistance Front (TRF). The charges: - Framed under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 and the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA), 1967 - Includes the grave penal section for 'waging war against India' - Filed in continuation of the original 1,597-page chargesheet dated 15 December 2025 Key strategy — charging outfits as legal entities: LeT and TRF have been charged as legal entities for planning, facilitating and executing the attack. This strengthens the evidentiary case internationally and helps in designating the outfits and pursuing them through global forums. Other accused: Pakistani handler Sajid Jatt and three terrorists (killed in Operation Mahadev, July 2025) were named earlier; two arrested civilians allegedly provided logistics. Institutions involved: The NIA is India's central counter-terror investigation agency, created by the NIA Act, 2008 after the 26/11 Mumbai attacks, empowered to probe scheduled offences across States. The UAPA, 1967 is the principal anti-terror law allowing designation of individuals/organisations as terrorists and extended detention. TRF is a proxy floated to give indigenous cover to Pakistan-sponsored terror in J&K.
prelimsFacts: NIA created by the NIA Act, 2008 (post-26/11)
UAPA 1967 — principal anti-terror law; allows designating individuals/orgs as terrorists
BNS 2023 replaced the IPC, retains 'waging war against the State'
TRF is a proxy/offshoot of LeT
Pahalgam attack: 22 April 2025, 26 civilians killed
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: Cross-border terrorism remains India's foremost internal security challenge. Examine the legal and institutional framework India has built to counter it, and the value of prosecuting terror outfits as legal entities.

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements: 1. The National Investigation Agency (NIA) was created by an Act of Parliament in 2008. 2. The UAPA, 1967 allows the government to designate individuals, and not just organisations, as terrorists. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: The NIA Act, 2008 was enacted after the 26/11 attacks, and the UAPA (post its 2019 amendment) permits designating individuals as terrorists.
prelimsQs.ref: Prelims facts: NIA Act 2008; UAPA designation power

prelimsQs.q: 'The Resistance Front (TRF)', sometimes seen in the news, is best described as:
prelimsQs.explanation: TRF is a proxy/offshoot of the Pakistan-based LeT, named in the Pahalgam attack chargesheet.
prelimsQs.ref: Article: TRF described as LeT proxy

2026-07-07.headline: Census 2027 Test Run Has 'Open Column' to Record Caste
2026-07-07.summary: The rehearsal for the second phase of Census 2027, begun in 16 States/UTs, carries an 'open column' for respondents to record their caste — signalling how caste is likely to be enumerated in the Population Census 2027, the first to count caste in independent India. Until now only SCs and STs were enumerated.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The caste census is a landmark governance and social-justice decision with direct bearing on welfare targeting and reservation policy — a very high-yield GS 1 and GS 2 topic.

deepDive.explanation: The pre-test for Census 2027 introduced an 'open column' for respondents to record their caste — a strong signal that Population Census 2027 will be the first to count caste in independent India (beyond SC/ST). How enumeration will work: - The Population Enumeration (PE) phase will record the castes of all residents; earlier only SCs and STs were counted - PE conducted in February 2027 nationwide (by 30 September in snowbound Ladakh/J&K/Uttarakhand/HP) - The final questionnaire will be known by September - SC/ST are recorded via codes; others get an open column for the enumerator to enter caste as stated - The pre-test (Ward No. 24, Faridabad, 1–20 July) allows self-enumeration The methodology debate — open column vs fixed list: - The 2011 SECC's open-column method returned over 46 lakh distinct 'caste names', which the government called unreliable - The 1931 Census (last comprehensive caste count) recorded 4,147 castes - Bihar's 2022-23 survey used a fixed caste list instead The choice will decide data quality: a standardised methodology is essential so the count informs welfare and reservation policy rather than producing unusable data. Concepts: The Census of India is conducted decennially under the Census Act, 1948 by the Registrar General & Census Commissioner (RGI), in two phases (Houselisting and Population Enumeration). The SECC 2011 (Socio-Economic and Caste Census) was conducted separately; its caste data were never fully released.
prelimsFacts: Census conducted under the Census Act, 1948 by the RGI
Two phases: Houselisting + Population Enumeration
PE for Census 2027: February 2027 (Sept 2027 in snowbound regions)
SECC 2011 open column returned 46+ lakh caste names
1931 Census recorded 4,147 castes
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: A caste census can sharpen welfare targeting but risks reinforcing caste identities. Critically examine the case for enumerating caste in Census 2027 and the methodological challenges involved.

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements about the Census in India: 1. It is conducted under the Census Act, 1948. 2. It is carried out in two phases — Houselisting and Population Enumeration. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: The Census is conducted by the RGI under the Census Act, 1948 in two phases.
prelimsQs.ref: Prelims facts: Census Act; two phases

prelimsQs.q: The last Census of India to enumerate all castes (beyond SCs/STs) was conducted in:
prelimsQs.explanation: The 1931 Census was the last full caste enumeration, recording 4,147 castes; since Independence only SCs and STs have been counted.

2026-07-07.headline: Skyroot's Vikram-1: India's First Private Orbital Rocket to Carry Six Payloads
2026-07-07.summary: Skyroot Aerospace announced that Vikram-1, India's first privately developed orbital-class rocket, will carry six payloads, with launch expected between 12 July and 4 August. Payloads include tech demonstrators from Graaha Space, Germany's Dcubed, Skyroot's SCOPE, and a space-debris capture experiment.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Private participation in space is a flagship reform story testing IN-SPACe, the space economy and indigenisation — core GS 3 Science & Technology.

deepDive.explanation: Skyroot Aerospace announced that Vikram-1, India's first privately developed orbital-class rocket, will carry six payloads, with launch expected between 12 July and 4 August. The payloads: - Technology demonstrators from Graaha Space (nano-satellite SOLARAS), Dcubed (Germany), and Skyroot's own SCOPE - Embrace — a space-debris capture experiment by Cosmoserve Space - A robotic arm stays attached to the payload deck for an in-space demonstration - Tribute payloads: Cosmic Bloom (diamond jewellery on an aluminium plate) and Microart — an 18K gold rocket holding micro-sculptures (smaller than a grain of rice) of C.V. Raman, Vikram Sarabhai and A.P.J. Abdul Kalam The rocket is named after Vikram Sarabhai, father of India's space programme. Why it matters: - Skyroot is a Hyderabad-based startup; in 2022 its Vikram-S became India's first privately built rocket to reach space (a sub-orbital flight, Mission Prarambh) - An orbital-class rocket can place payloads into orbit (unlike sub-orbital flights that only cross into space and return) — a major capability leap - IN-SPACe (under the Department of Space) is the single-window nodal agency that authorises and promotes private participation - Space-debris removal is an emerging capability to de-orbit defunct satellites/junk, key to sustainable orbital use
prelimsFacts: Vikram-1: India's first private orbital-class rocket (by Skyroot)
Vikram-S (2022): first private Indian rocket to reach space (sub-orbital, Mission Prarambh)
IN-SPACe: single-window nodal agency under DoS for private space
Named after Vikram Sarabhai
Embrace payload = space-debris capture experiment
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: Private participation is transforming India's space sector. Discuss the role of IN-SPACe and private launch vehicles like Vikram-1 in building India's space economy.

prelimsQs.q: Vikram-1, recently in the news, is:
options: India's first privately developed orbital-class rocket
prelimsQs.explanation: Vikram-1 by Skyroot Aerospace is India's first private orbital-class rocket; Vikram-S (2022) was the earlier sub-orbital flight.

prelimsQs.q: IN-SPACe, often seen in the news with private space launches, functions under which of the following?
prelimsQs.explanation: IN-SPACe is the single-window nodal agency under the Department of Space to promote and authorise private-sector space activity.

2026-07-07.headline: In India, Voting Cannot Remain Merely a Statutory Right
2026-07-07.summary: A demand to recognise voting as a fundamental right revived an old debate. For seven decades the Supreme Court has held voting a statutory right, yet its own rulings have progressively constitutionalised many facets of voting — creating an anomaly a former CEC argues the Court should revisit.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The nature of the right to vote links Fundamental Rights, elections and the basic structure doctrine — a classic conceptual GS 2 Polity question.

deepDive.explanation: A Congress leader's demand to recognise voting as a fundamental right revived a long-standing debate. For seven decades the Supreme Court has held voting to be a statutory right, yet its rulings have progressively constitutionalised many facets of voting. The traditional view (statutory right): - N.P. Ponnuswami (1952) and Jyoti Basu (1982) held the right to vote/contest are statutory, not fundamental - Reaffirmed in Kuldip Nayar (2006) The counter-trend (constitutionalisation): - ADR (2002): voters' right to know candidates' antecedents under Article 19(1)(a) - PUCL (2003): freedom of informed voting is fundamental - NOTA judgment (2013): right to reject all candidates and ballot secrecy protected under Article 19(1)(a) - Anoop Baranwal (2023): Justice Rastogi favoured recognising voting as a fundamental right Article 326 mandates elections on universal adult suffrage — every citizen 18+ is constitutionally entitled to be registered, subject to narrow disqualifications. The argument: Since democracy and free and fair elections are part of the basic structure (Kesavananda Bharati, 1973; Indira Nehru Gandhi, 1975), the citizen's core right to vote cannot remain a 'constitutional orphan' — if the Constitution protects the right to reject all candidates, it should protect the right to choose one. Key distinction: A statutory right flows from ordinary legislation (amendable by Parliament); a fundamental right (Part III) is constitutionally guaranteed and enforceable under Article 32.
prelimsFacts: Statutory-right view: N.P. Ponnuswami (1952), Jyoti Basu (1982), Kuldip Nayar (2006)
ADR (2002) & PUCL (2003): right to know / informed voting under Art 19(1)(a)
NOTA judgment (2013): right to reject, secrecy under Art 19(1)(a)
Article 326: universal adult suffrage
Basic structure: Kesavananda Bharati (1973)
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'The right to vote in India is caught between statutory status and constitutional protection.' Critically examine the evolution of the Supreme Court's position and the case for recognising voting as a fundamental right.

prelimsQs.q: Under the Constitution of India, universal adult suffrage for elections is provided by:
prelimsQs.explanation: Article 326 provides that elections to the Lok Sabha and State Assemblies shall be on the basis of adult suffrage.

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements: 1. The Supreme Court has consistently treated the right to vote as a fundamental right. 2. The right of voters to know the antecedents of candidates has been read into Article 19(1)(a). Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: The Court has long held voting to be a statutory right (Ponnuswami 1952; Kuldip Nayar 2006), while the right to know (ADR 2002, PUCL 2003) flows from Article 19(1)(a).

2026-07-07.headline: Supreme Court Declares 'Right to Walk' on Footpaths a Fundamental Right
2026-07-07.summary: The Supreme Court ruled the Right to Walk safely on footpaths a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(d) (freedom of movement) and Article 21 (right to life), giving pedestrians a claim on public pathways over automobiles — reframing urban mobility as a rights question.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: An example of the judicial expansion of Article 21, and a fresh angle on urban planning and pedestrian safety — useful across GS 2 (rights) and GS 1 (urban society).

deepDive.explanation: The Supreme Court ruled that the Right to Walk safely on footpaths is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(d) (freedom of movement) and Article 21 (right to life), giving pedestrians a claim on public pathways over motorised vehicles. Key facts: - Judgment delivered by Justices P.S. Narasimha and A.S. Chandurkar - Places pedestrians' claim to safe walking space above motorised traffic on public roads - The accompanying op-ed situates walking in India's political history — Gandhi's Dandi March, Subhas Chandra Bose's 'Delhi Chalo' — and cultural traditions Significance: The ruling reframes urban mobility as a rights question, obliging municipal authorities to provide safe, unobstructed, walkable public spaces — with implications for city planning, road design and pedestrian safety. The constitutional basis: - Article 21 (right to life and personal liberty) has been judicially expanded to include dignity, clean environment, health and now safe pedestrian movement - Article 19(1)(d) guarantees the right to move freely throughout India, subject to reasonable restrictions - This continues the expansive interpretation of Article 21 from Maneka Gandhi (1978) — that 'life' means a life of dignity, not mere animal existence
prelimsFacts: Right to Walk grounded in Article 19(1)(d) + Article 21
Bench: Justices P.S. Narasimha and A.S. Chandurkar
Continues Maneka Gandhi (1978) expansive reading of Article 21
Article 19(1)(d): freedom of movement throughout India
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: The judiciary has continually expanded the scope of Article 21. Using the 'Right to Walk' judgment as an illustration, discuss the merits and limits of such judicial expansion of fundamental rights.

prelimsQs.q: The Supreme Court grounded the newly declared 'Right to Walk' on footpaths in which Articles of the Constitution?
prelimsQs.explanation: The Court located the right in Article 19(1)(d) (freedom of movement) read with Article 21 (right to life).

prelimsQs.q: The expansive interpretation of Article 21 that underlies rulings like the 'Right to Walk' judgment traces back to which landmark case?
options: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
prelimsQs.explanation: Maneka Gandhi (1978) began the expansive reading of 'life and personal liberty' under Article 21.

2026-07-07.headline: AI Governance and a Voice for the Global South
2026-07-07.summary: After hosting the India AI Impact Summit 2026 to centre the Global South, India has drifted toward 'middle power' positioning and joined Pax Silica, aligning with the US-led semiconductor supply chain — at the cost, the op-ed argues, of Global-South solidarity and strategic autonomy.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: AI governance, strategic autonomy and Global-South leadership are converging into a major GS 2 IR theme, freshly relevant with the UN Global Dialogue on AI under way.

deepDive.explanation: After hosting the India AI Impact Summit 2026 (February) to centre the Global South in AI discourse, India has drifted toward a 'middle power' positioning and joined Pax Silica, aligning with the US-led semiconductor supply chain — at the cost, the authors argue, of Global-South solidarity and strategic autonomy. Key points: - The Summit was a departure from Bletchley 2023, Seoul 2024, Paris 2025 (which stressed catastrophic/existential risk) — it foregrounded present harms, equity and inclusion - By joining Pax Silica, India adopted a 'pro-innovation' regulatory approach, which the authors say compromises strategic autonomy - Risks flagged: India as a consumer of US tech bearing disproportionate harm; a site of data/labour/mineral extraction; land for data centres displacing communities - The first part of the UN Global Dialogue on AI is under way in Geneva (6–7 July 2026) Way forward: India should use the Geneva dialogue to lead a fractured global AI-governance agenda — asserting a vision rooted in public purpose, user safety, strategic autonomy and Global-South cooperation (pooling data, compute, standards and regulatory capacity) as a counterweight to Big Tech hegemony. Concepts: A middle power shapes outcomes through coalitions rather than dominance. Strategic autonomy is India's policy of independent decision-making, avoiding binding alignment with any bloc.
prelimsFacts: India AI Impact Summit 2026 held in February; centred the Global South
Prior AI summits: Bletchley 2023, Seoul 2024, Paris 2025
Pax Silica = US-led semiconductor supply-chain alignment
UN Global Dialogue on AI: Geneva, 6–7 July 2026
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'AI governance is emerging as a new arena of North-South contestation.' Examine how India can balance strategic autonomy with the interests of the Global South in shaping global AI rules.

prelimsQs.q: Arrange the following global AI summits in the correct chronological order: 1. Seoul Summit 2. Bletchley Park Summit 3. Paris Summit 4. India AI Impact Summit
prelimsQs.explanation: Bletchley (2023) → Seoul (2024) → Paris (2025) → India AI Impact Summit (Feb 2026).

prelimsQs.q: The term 'Pax Silica', recently seen in commentary on technology geopolitics, refers to:
options: A UN treaty on semiconductor trade
A US-led alignment of the semiconductor supply chain
prelimsQs.explanation: 'Pax Silica' denotes the US-led effort to align global semiconductor supply chains with trusted partners.

2026-07-07.headline: El Niño to Strain India's Power System — CREA Study
2026-07-07.summary: CREA warns India's power system will be strained more by the developing El Niño than any other country's, as weaker wind/hydropower and rising AC demand could open a generation gap of ~18 TWh over the year to June 2027 — likely filled by coal, releasing ~17 million tonnes of CO2.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Links climate variability, the energy transition and grid reliability — a rich GS 3 topic combining El Niño, renewables, BESS and coal dependence.

deepDive.explanation: The think tank CREA (Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air) warns that India's power system stands to be strained more by the developing El Niño than any other country's, as weaker wind/hydropower and rising air-conditioning demand could open a generation gap of nearly 18 TWh over the year to June 2027. Key numbers: - Projected median shortfall 17.7 TWh (severe case 24 TWh) — under 1% of India's ~1,846 billion units generated in 2025-26 - Likeliest fill is a surge in coal power, releasing ~17 million tonnes of CO2 - Non-fossil sources supplied 29.2% of generation; non-fossil installed capacity reached 283.46 GW (solar 150.26 GW, wind 56.09 GW, large hydro 51.41 GW, nuclear 8.78 GW) - Coal is ~42% of installed capacity; peak demand hit 270.82 GW on 21 May - Grid operators curtailed ~2.1 TWh of solar/wind to keep coal running — avoidable, CREA says, with ~10 GWh of battery storage. India is lining up ~130 GW of new coal capacity Way forward: CREA urges India to 'move much faster on batteries and grid upgrades' (BESS) so clean energy can meet demand surges instead of defaulting to coal. Concepts: El Niño — Pacific warming that suppresses the Indian monsoon and raises temperatures, cutting hydro/wind output while boosting cooling demand. BESS — Battery Energy Storage System, storing surplus renewable power for peaks. Curtailment — deliberate reduction of available renewable generation when the grid cannot absorb it.
prelimsFacts: Projected El Niño power shortfall: 17.7 TWh median (24 TWh severe)
Non-fossil = 29.2% of generation; capacity 283.46 GW
Coal ~42% of installed capacity; peak demand 270.82 GW (21 May)
1 TWh = 1 billion units (kWh)
BESS stores renewable surplus to cut curtailment & coal use
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: El Niño exposes the fragility of India's renewable-heavy grid during demand surges. Discuss how battery storage and grid modernisation can reconcile energy reliability with India's climate commitments.

prelimsQs.q: With reference to India's electricity system, consider the following statements: 1. Non-fossil sources account for a little under one-third of actual electricity generation. 2. One terawatt-hour (TWh) equals one billion units (kWh) of electricity. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: Non-fossil sources contribute about 29.2% of generation, and 1 TWh = 1 billion kWh (units).

prelimsQs.q: During an El Niño year, India's power system is stressed mainly because:
options: Solar output collapses due to cloud cover
Wind and hydro generation weaken while cooling demand rises
Coal plants must legally reduce output
Transmission losses double in dry weather
prelimsQs.explanation: El Niño weakens monsoon winds and inflows (hurting wind/hydro) while heat raises AC demand, widening the generation gap.

2026-07-07.headline: NSE Targets Rs 30,000-crore IPO in September — India's Largest Ever
2026-07-07.summary: The National Stock Exchange (NSE) is targeting its long-awaited IPO, estimated at around Rs 30,000 crore, in September — set to be India's largest ever, surpassing Hyundai Motor India's Rs 27,870-crore IPO of October 2024.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Capital markets, IPOs and SEBI regulation are staple GS 3 Economy topics; the largest-ever listing is a fact worth remembering for Prelims.

deepDive.explanation: The National Stock Exchange (NSE) is targeting its long-awaited initial public offering (IPO), estimated at around Rs 30,000 crore, in September — set to be India's largest ever. Key facts: - Would surpass Hyundai Motor India's Rs 27,870-crore IPO (October 2024) as the biggest in India - NSE is valued at over Rs 5 lakh crore in the unlisted market; roadshows to begin soon - The listing had long been pending amid regulatory scrutiny by SEBI Concepts: - An IPO is the first sale of a company's shares to the public, listing it on a stock exchange - The NSE is India's largest stock exchange by turnover and home of the Nifty 50 index - SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) is the statutory regulator of securities markets under the SEBI Act, 1992, which vets IPO disclosures

Previous largest: Hyundai Motor India, Rs 27,870 crore (Oct 2024)

prelimsQs.explanation: SEBI, under the SEBI Act 1992, regulates public issues and vets IPO disclosures.

prelimsQs.q: Prior to the proposed NSE issue, India's largest IPO so far has been that of:

2026-07-07.headline: Ministry of Cooperation at 5 Years: Cooperative Life Insurer, Tribhuvan University
2026-07-07.summary: Marking the fifth Foundation Day of the Ministry of Cooperation, Union Minister Amit Shah announced a cooperative life insurance company and other measures. India has ~8.5 lakh cooperatives with over 30 crore members; the 'Tribhuvan' Sahkari University is being set up at Anand, Gujarat.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Cooperatives are a growing governance and rural-economy focus ('Sahkar se Samriddhi'); the Ministry, IFFCO and the new university are Prelims-worthy facts.

deepDive.explanation: Marking the fifth Foundation Day of the Ministry of Cooperation, Union Cooperation Minister Amit Shah announced a cooperative life insurance company and other measures, saying the Ministry gave a 'lifeline' to India's cooperative movement. Key facts: - India has ~8.5 lakh cooperatives with over 30 crore members - Bharat Taxi, a cooperative-based ride-hailing platform, to expand to 500 cities in two years; fertilizer co-op IFFCO is already in insurance via a Japanese JV - The 'Tribhuvan' Sahkari University is being set up at Anand, Gujarat for cooperative human-resource development - Shah transferred 135 grain godowns (75,000-tonne capacity), inaugurated 85, and laid foundations for 47 storage godowns Concepts: - The Ministry of Cooperation (2021) was created to realise 'Sahkar se Samriddhi'; cooperation is a State subject (Entry 32, State List), so the Ministry frames policy without interfering in States - IFFCO (Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative) is one of the world's largest cooperatives - Tribhuvan Sahkari University is named after Tribhuvandas Patel, founder of the Amul (Kaira district) dairy cooperative
prelimsFacts: Ministry of Cooperation created 2021 ('Sahkar se Samriddhi')
Cooperation is a State subject — Entry 32, State List
India: ~8.5 lakh cooperatives, 30+ crore members
Tribhuvan Sahkari University at Anand, Gujarat (named after Tribhuvandas Patel, Amul founder)
IFFCO — among world's largest cooperatives

prelimsQs.explanation: Cooperative societies fall under Entry 32 of the State List, and the Ministry of Cooperation was created in July 2021.

prelimsQs.q: India's first national cooperative university, 'Tribhuvan Sahkari University', is being established at:

2026-07-07.headline: Questions Surrounding the Quad's Future
2026-07-07.summary: The US renamed its Indo-Pacific Command back to Pacific Command and its Nov 2025 National Security Strategy mentioned the Quad only in passing — reviving questions about the grouping's relevance. Its cooperation list has grown to 37 areas, but the pledged $20 billion Critical Minerals Initiative remains unfunded.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The Quad, Indo-Pacific and India-US-China dynamics are perennial GS 2 IR themes; the debate over the Quad's security vs civilian focus is analytically rich.

deepDive.explanation: The US renamed its Indo-Pacific Command back to Pacific Command on 16 June (reversing a 2018 change), and its November 2025 National Security Strategy prioritised the Western Hemisphere and mentioned the Quad only in passing — reviving questions about the grouping's relevance. Criticisms: - Reluctance to name China; hesitation to be security-centric (members distinguish 'Quad' from the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue/QSD, rooted in the first 2004 QSD) - No elevation to a 2+2 format Where it stands: - The Quad's cooperation list has grown to 37 areas, 23 security-related; focus on interoperability, data-sharing, monitoring and relief (civilian/non-military grade with dual-use potential) - Funding skews to health and climate; the pledged $20 billion Critical Minerals Initiative remains an unfunded target Way forward: The Quad's future 'will not be settled by nomenclature in Washington' but by whether it backs its security-heavy agenda with capital — especially for resilient supply chains in critical minerals, technology and maritime domains where contestation with China is sharpest. Concepts: The Quad (India, US, Japan, Australia) is a diplomatic/strategic grouping for a free and open Indo-Pacific, revived in 2017 after the original 2007 grouping lapsed. The QSD (2004) grew from Indian Ocean tsunami-relief coordination. A 2+2 dialogue is a joint meeting of Foreign and Defence Ministers.
prelimsFacts: Quad members: India, US, Japan, Australia; revived 2017 (original 2007)
QSD origin: 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami-relief coordination
US Indo-Pacific Command renamed Pacific Command (16 June)
Quad cooperation: 37 areas, 23 security-related
Critical Minerals Initiative: $20 billion pledged, still unfunded
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'The Quad's relevance will be decided by delivery, not nomenclature.' Critically examine the challenges facing the grouping and its significance for India's Indo-Pacific strategy.

prelimsQs.q: The 'Quad' grouping comprises which of the following countries?
prelimsQs.explanation: The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue includes India, the US, Japan and Australia.

prelimsQs.q: The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue traces its origin to cooperation among the four navies during:
options: The 2008 Gulf of Aden anti-piracy patrols
prelimsQs.explanation: Coordination for 2004 tsunami relief seeded the Quad idea, formalised in 2007 and revived in 2017.

2026-07-07.headline: Will El Niño Weaken India's Economy?
2026-07-07.summary: With IMD forecasting below-normal July rainfall after a ~40% June deficit and warnings of a potential 'super El Niño', analysts assess how a weak monsoon could depress kharif output, rural incomes, growth and exports while stoking food inflation. A poor monsoon could shave 20-65 bps off GDP growth.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Monsoon-economy linkages, agriculture's role in GVA vs employment, and inflation are core GS 3 Economy — recurrent in both Mains and Prelims.

deepDive.explanation: With IMD forecasting below-normal July rainfall after a ~40% June deficit, and Agriculture Minister Chouhan warning of a potential 'super El Niño', analysts assess how a weak monsoon could depress kharif output, rural incomes, growth and exports while stoking food inflation. Three transmission channels: A poor monsoon hits the economy via lower agricultural output, dented rural demand, and higher food prices. Key numbers: - 2024-25 foodgrain output hit a record 357.73 million tonnes; farm incomes could fall up to 10% - A combined El Niño-plus-drought could shave 20–65 basis points off GDP growth (Kotak) - Agriculture is ~one-fifth of GVA but employs 46% of the workforce and supports ~55% of the population - The Cabinet approved a Rs 41,533-crore Nutrient-Based Subsidy for P&K fertilizers (28 grades) for kharif; shortfalls could widen the Current Account Deficit - Worst droughts fell in El Niño years — 1972, 1982, 2009, 2015; 315 districts are vulnerable, 111 of primary concern Way forward: Experts urge moving 'beyond crop insurance to ex-ante risk reduction' — public investment in irrigation, drought-resistant high-yielding seeds and water storage — to 'drought-proof' the economy, since a second successive weak monsoon would be far more damaging. Concepts: GVA measures output by sector (agriculture's small GVA share belies its huge employment role). NBS is a fixed per-nutrient subsidy on P&K fertilizers. CAD widens when imports cover shortfalls. Ex-ante risk reduction cuts risk before a shock (vs ex-post relief).

2024-25 foodgrain output: record 357.73 million tonnes
Agriculture: ~1/5 of GVA but 46% of workforce
El Niño drought years: 1972, 1982, 2009, 2015
Rs 41,533-crore Nutrient-Based Subsidy for P&K (kharif)

2026-07-07.headline: Can Bar Associations Refuse to Represent an Accused?
2026-07-07.summary: The Faizabad Bar Association's resolution not to represent the accused in the Ayodhya Ram Temple embezzlement case raised whether a Bar body can collectively refuse legal representation — which the Supreme Court has repeatedly held illegal, as it violates the right to counsel and fair trial.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The right to legal representation ties together Articles 22(1), 39A, 14 and 21 and legal ethics — a conceptually clean GS 2 Polity/judiciary question.

deepDive.explanation: The Faizabad Bar Association's resolution not to represent the accused in the Ayodhya Ram Temple embezzlement case raised the question of whether a Bar body can collectively refuse legal representation — which the Supreme Court has repeatedly held illegal. Key facts: - In A.S. Mohammed Rafi v. State of TN (2010), the SC held such Bar resolutions 'wholly illegal, against all traditions and professional ethics' and null and void - Article 22(1) guarantees an arrested person the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of choice; Article 14 and Article 21 reinforce it; Article 39A (DPSP) mandates free legal aid - BCI Rules bind advocates to accept briefs; 'special circumstances' for refusal apply to the individual advocate, not the Bar (Kuldeep Agarwal, 2019) - Precedents: refusals for Ajmal Kasab (26/11), the 2012 Delhi gangrape and 2019 Hyderabad cases Way forward: Courts have consistently upheld legal representation as a core feature of a fair trial; right-minded lawyers should 'defy such resolutions' so that the rule of law and access to justice are preserved. Concepts: Article 22(1) — right to counsel of choice; Article 39A — DPSP requiring free legal aid and equal access to justice; Bar Council of India (BCI) — statutory body under the Advocates Act, 1961; Fair trial — a facet of Article 21 balancing the accused, victim and society.
prelimsFacts: A.S. Mohammed Rafi v. State of TN (2010): Bar refusal resolutions illegal
Article 22(1): right to counsel of choice on arrest
Article 39A (DPSP): free legal aid
BCI under the Advocates Act, 1961
Refusal precedents: Ajmal Kasab, 2012 Delhi & 2019 Hyderabad cases
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'Every accused, however heinous the charge, has a right to legal defence.' Discuss with reference to constitutional provisions and the professional ethics of advocates.

prelimsQs.q: The right of an arrested person to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of his choice is guaranteed under:
prelimsQs.explanation: Article 22(1) guarantees the right to counsel of one's choice upon arrest.

prelimsQs.q: Free legal aid to ensure justice is not denied due to economic disability is provided for in the Constitution under:
prelimsQs.explanation: Article 39A, a Directive Principle inserted by the 42nd Amendment, mandates free legal aid.

2026-07-07.whyItMatters: UN institutions, UNSC reform and India's Global-South diplomacy are central GS 2 IR themes; the India-Tajikistan-OIC contest is a topical case study.

Jaishankar in New York — a bid seen as central to India's Global-South leadership. The contest: - Tajikistan is the rival candidate for the Asia-Pacific seat; it has secured the backing of the 57-member OIC bloc - India has support from Fiji, the US, Austria and Sri Lanka - Both India and Tajikistan are SCO members; the next SCO summit is in Kyrgyzstan - India earlier postponed the India-Africa Forum Summit (28–31 May) citing the Ebola outbreak Way forward: Winning the seat will require heavy diplomatic mobilisation to offset the OIC bloc's numbers; it advances India's long-standing push for UNSC reform and permanent membership. Concepts: - The UNSC is the UN's peace-and-security organ — 5 permanent (P5, with veto) and 10 non-permanent members elected for two-year terms by the General Assembly, with seats allotted by region - OIC — Organisation of Islamic Cooperation, a 57-state bloc whose voting weight matters in UN elections - SCO — Shanghai Cooperation Organisation, a Eurasian security grouping including India, China, Russia, Pakistan and Central Asian states

2026-07-07.headline: Mumbai's Monsoon Flooding: Infrastructure Falling Behind
2026-07-07.summary: Intense monsoon rain paralysed Mumbai and parts of Maharashtra, killing at least seven, shutting the Mumbai-Pune Expressway and rail links. Editorials argue drainage upgrades alone cannot withstand climate-driven cloudbursts, and cities must shift to absorptive 'Sponge City' design.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Urban flooding blends urbanisation, disaster management and climate adaptation — a GS 1 and GS 3 theme, with BRIMSTOWAD and Sponge City as key concepts.

deepDive.explanation: Intense monsoon rain paralysed Mumbai and parts of Maharashtra, killing at least seven (a chawl collapse in Mankhurd killed five children; a Pune landslide killed three), shutting the Mumbai-Pune Expressway and rail links. Both editorials argue drainage upgrades alone cannot withstand climate-driven cloudbursts. Why Mumbai floods: - Built on reclaimed land (former seven islands), low-lying — flooding worsens when heavy rain coincides with high tide (seawater backs up the gravity-based stormwater outfalls) - After the July 2005 floods (944 mm in 24 hours), Mumbai launched BRIMSTOWAD (drain-widening, pumping stations, pre-monsoon de-silting) — still incomplete - Accountability is split across BMC, IMD, NDRF, two Railway zones, State and highway authorities - IE cites China's Sponge City policy (post-2012 Beijing floods) and the Netherlands' water-retention urbanism as models Way forward: Cities must shift from reactive drainage fixes to climate-proof, absorptive urban design — permeable surfaces, restored wetlands/creeks, water-retention (Sponge City) — since 'waiting for system capacity to catch up to demand will be a failing strategy.' Concepts: BRIMSTOWAD — Brihanmumbai Storm Water Drain project, Mumbai's post-2005 drainage overhaul. Sponge City — urban design that absorbs, stores and reuses rainwater. NDRF — National Disaster Response Force under the DM Act, 2005. Cloudburst — sudden, very heavy localised rainfall (often >100 mm/hour).

2005 floods: 944 mm in 24 hours → BRIMSTOWAD launched
Sponge City model: China (post-2012 Beijing floods)
NDRF under the Disaster Management Act, 2005
Cloudburst: often >100 mm/hour localised rain

prelimsQs.explanation: China adopted the Sponge City programme after the 2012 Beijing floods, emphasising absorptive urban design.

2026-07-07.headline: A Defection Wave in Tamil Nadu
2026-07-07.summary: Since the Tamilaga Vetri Kazhagam (TVK), led by actor C. Joseph Vijay, came to power, several MLAs — mostly from the AIADMK — resigned seats to join the ruling party, hoping for renomination in by-elections. The Opposition calls it 'horse-trading' that sidesteps the anti-defection law.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The resign-and-recontest workaround exposes a persistent loophole in the Tenth Schedule — a live GS 2 Polity debate on defection and democratic ethics.

deepDive.explanation: Since the Tamilaga Vetri Kazhagam (TVK), led by actor C. Joseph Vijay, came to power, several MLAs — mostly from the AIADMK — have resigned seats to join the ruling party, hoping for renomination in by-elections, in a phenomenon the Opposition calls 'horse-trading'. Key facts: - The TVK emerged the single-largest party but fell 10 short of a majority; it formed government after the Congress switched from the DMK camp, with CPI/CPI(M)/VCK support - 25 MLAs backed TVK in the floor test; six AIADMK legislators have quit and joined TVK - Cross-overs include senior AIADMK figures who judge the AIADMK-DMK bipolar order to be ending - Counter-allegations: DMK accuses TVK of inducing its MLAs; TVK alleges a Rs 35-crore lure of its MLA Way forward: The resignation-and-recontest route sidesteps the spirit of the anti-defection law (resigning avoids disqualification under the Tenth Schedule); communist parties warn it undermines democratic norms — echoing the need to plug Tenth Schedule loopholes seen in Maharashtra. Concepts: The Tenth Schedule (Anti-Defection Law, 52nd Amendment, 1985) disqualifies legislators who voluntarily give up party membership or defy the whip — but resigning the seat and recontesting is a workaround. A by-election fills a seat vacated mid-term; a floor test proves a government's majority.

2026-07-07.headline: Tamil Nadu CM Opposes Per-Capita Amendment to Food Security Act
2026-07-07.summary: TN CM urged PM Modi to retain the existing 35 kg per-household monthly entitlement under Antyodaya Anna Yojana, opposing a proposed NFSA amendment to a per-capita basis (7 kg/person, capped at 35 kg/household) that would cut grain for smaller families and penalise southern States.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Food security, the NFSA and Centre-State welfare tensions are core GS 2; the household-vs-per-capita design question is a sharp federalism-and-welfare debate.

deepDive.explanation: Tamil Nadu CM C. Joseph Vijay urged PM Modi to retain the existing 35 kg foodgrain per household per month entitlement under Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY), opposing a proposed amendment to Section 3(1) of the NFSA, 2013 that would shift to a per-capita basis. The proposed change: 7 kg per person per month, capped at 35 kg per household — which would cut grain for smaller families. The State's argument: - TN's average family size is 3.54; the State has 18,64,600 AAY cards covering 69,26,983 beneficiaries - It would penalise States with smaller families, especially southern States that implemented family planning successfully — hitting nearly 70 lakh vulnerable citizens - AAY covers the poorest — widows, disabled, elderly without income, tribals, landless labourers The balancing question: The Centre says the change removes intra-category inequities and aligns with nutritional needs; the State counters it undermines the NFSA's unconditional, household-based safety net — a federalism-and-welfare balancing question. Concepts: The NFSA, 2013 gives ~two-thirds of the population a legal right to subsidised foodgrains via the PDS, in two categories — AAY (poorest, 35 kg/household) and Priority Households (PHH) (5 kg/person). AAY was launched in 2000 for the 'poorest of the poor'. Section 3, NFSA specifies entitlement quantum and price.

2026-07-07.headline: Democratic Lessons on Election Boycott for the Opposition
2026-07-07.summary: Amid whispers of an Opposition election boycott over perceived unfairness (SIR, delimitation, party splits), Yogendra Yadav's op-ed argues 'election boycott is a good question but a bad answer' — drawing on democratic theory and Global-South experience to urge contesting rather than ceding the field.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Electoral integrity, boycott ethics and the basic structure of free-and-fair elections make for a strong analytical GS 2 Polity essay/answer.

deepDive.explanation: Amid whispers of an election boycott by the Opposition over perceived unfairness (SIR, delimitation, party splits), Yogendra Yadav's op-ed argues that 'election boycott is a good question but a bad answer' — drawing on democratic theory and Global-South experience. Key arguments: - Cites Adam Przeworski: competitive elections are worth it because they let divided societies transfer power peacefully (as in India's 1977 post-Emergency election) - Uses the Pippa Norris (2014) framework of 11 dimensions of electoral integrity, arguing Indian elections have slipped on campaign finance, neutral authorities, transparency, voter registration and constituency boundaries - Three reasons to reject boycott: an ethical (communicative) argument; a strategic one (boycotts rarely dent turnout enough to hurt autocrats; elections offer organising space); and a historical one (mass upsurges — e.g., Hungary, 1977 India — can still topple entrenched regimes) Way forward: The relevant test for a boycott is 'not the extent of unfairness but the availability of spaces to expose this unfairness'; the Opposition should contest to document fraud, build public opinion and prepare for a democratic wave rather than cede the field. Concepts: Electoral integrity — how well an election meets international fairness standards. SIR (Special Intensive Revision) — the ECI's house-to-house electoral-roll revision. Delimitation — redrawing constituency boundaries/seat allocation. Free and fair elections are part of the basic structure.
prelimsFacts: Pippa Norris (2014): 11 dimensions of electoral integrity
SIR = ECI's Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls
Delimitation = redrawing constituency boundaries/seats
Free and fair elections = basic structure
1977 election: peaceful transfer after the Emergency

2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The EBP programme links energy security, emissions, agriculture and consumer protection — a multi-dimensional GS 3 Economy/Environment topic.

deepDive.explanation: India's push to E20 (20% ethanol-blended petrol) — achieved five years ahead of the 2030 target — is running into consumer opposition over fuel-economy loss and engine wear in older vehicles. Key facts: - India fully transitioned to E20 last year; E20 (80% petrol + 20% ethanol) is now the standard petrol nationwide — blending doubled from 10% to 20% in about three years (originally slated over eight) - Three problems for engines not designed for it: a drop in fuel economy, corrosion/part damage (ethanol is hygroscopic — absorbs water), and grievances of older-vehicle (E10-certified) owners - Chemistry: ethanol (C2H5OH, 2 carbon atoms) emits less CO2 than petrol (8–12 carbon hydrocarbons), but has a lower energy content (hence lower mileage) Way forward: The government must balance the energy-security and emissions gains of ethanol (cutting oil imports, using surplus grain/sugar) against consumer-end costs; clearer labelling, compensation for efficiency loss, and E20-compatible engine standards are needed to sustain public buy-in. Concepts: The Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme blends ethanol (from sugarcane/molasses and increasingly grain) with petrol to cut crude imports and emissions. Hygroscopy — tendency to attract water, causing corrosion. Flex-fuel vehicles — engines designed for high blends (up to E85), a longer-term fix. Energy content — ethanol yields less energy per litre than petrol.
prelimsFacts: E20 = 80% petrol + 20% ethanol; now standard nationwide
E20 achieved ~5 years ahead of the 2030 target
Ethanol is hygroscopic (absorbs water → corrosion)
Ethanol has lower energy content → lower mileage
Flex-fuel vehicles handle up to E85

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements about ethanol-blended petrol: 1. E20 denotes petrol blended with 20% ethanol. 2. Ethanol has a higher energy content than petrol, improving mileage. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: E20 is 20% ethanol; ethanol's LOWER energy content slightly reduces fuel economy.
prelimsQs.ref: Prelims facts: E20; lower energy content

prelimsQs.q: Older petrol vehicles face corrosion risk with high ethanol blends chiefly because ethanol is:
prelimsQs.explanation: Ethanol absorbs moisture, which corrodes fuel-system components not designed for it.

2026-07-07.headline: India Can Become a 'Connector Economy' — Anand Mahindra
2026-07-07.summary: In his letter to shareholders, Anand Mahindra argued the next global order will be shaped by 'connectors', and India is well placed to become a 'connector economy' linking rival blocs — leveraging democracy, a China buffer, a large market and trust across ideological camps.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Global value chains, China+1 and supply-chain resilience are hot GS 3 Economy themes; the 'connector economy' framing is a useful, quotable concept.

deepDive.explanation: In his letter to shareholders, Anand Mahindra (Chairman, Mahindra Group) argued that in a fragmented world the next global order will be shaped by 'connectors', and India is well placed to become a 'connector economy' that works across divides. India's advantages (per Mahindra): - Democracy and political stability - A geographic/economic buffer against China - A large internal market - Trust across ideological blocs ('goodwill towards all') Key ideas: - He argued 'black swan events have become obsolete because the pond is full of black swans' — episodic shocks have become a continuous reset of supply chains, geopolitical alignments and technologies - He framed the moment as a new 'Manthan 2.0', urging agility and resilience Way forward: As global supply chains reconfigure (China+1, friend-shoring), India can position itself as a trusted node linking rival blocs — provided it builds manufacturing depth, logistics and regulatory predictability to convert goodwill into investment. Concepts: A connector economy intermediates trade, capital and technology flows between rival blocs, leveraging trust and neutrality. A Global Value Chain (GVC) is the cross-border network of production stages. Black swan — a rare, high-impact, hard-to-predict event (N.N. Taleb). Friend-shoring — relocating supply chains to trusted/allied countries.
prelimsFacts: 'Connector economy' — intermediates flows between rival blocs
India's edge: democracy, China buffer, large market, cross-bloc trust
China+1 & friend-shoring reshaping global value chains
'Black swan' term coined by N.N. Taleb
Mahindra framed it as 'Manthan 2.0'

2026-07-07.headline: INS Mahendragiri: Sixth Project 17A Stealth Frigate to be Commissioned
2026-07-07.summary: The Indian Navy will commission its sixth Project 17A indigenous stealth frigate, Mahendragiri (F38), at Visakhapatnam on 11 July — designed by the Warship Design Bureau and built by Mazagon Dock, with over 75% indigenous content.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Indigenous warship-building is a flagship 'Aatmanirbhar' defence story; Project 17A, MDL and the Warship Design Bureau are Prelims-relevant GS 3 facts.

deepDive.explanation: The Indian Navy will commission its sixth Project 17A indigenous stealth frigate, Mahendragiri (F38), at Visakhapatnam on 11 July — designed by the Navy's Warship Design Bureau and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Ltd (MDL), Mumbai. Key features: - Advanced stealth (reduced radar signature), high automation and a combined diesel-or-gas (CODOG) propulsion system - Capable of anti-air, anti-surface and anti-submarine operations, plus HADR and maritime-security roles - Over 75% indigenous content, drawing on a network of MSMEs - Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats (Odisha) — the first warship to bear the name; six of seven Project 17A frigates are now commissioned Concepts: - Project 17A — a follow-on to the Shivalik-class (Project 17): seven guided-missile stealth frigates built by MDL and GRSE (Kolkata) under the Navy's indigenous warship programme - Stealth frigate — a warship designed to minimise radar, acoustic and infrared signatures - Warship Design Bureau — the Navy's in-house ship-design organisation, central to 'Aatmanirbhar' naval construction
prelimsFacts: INS Mahendragiri (F38): 6th Project 17A frigate
Designed by Warship Design Bureau; built by MDL, Mumbai
Project 17A frigates built by MDL & GRSE (Kolkata)
Over 75% indigenous content; CODOG propulsion

2026-07-07.headline: Polio Campaign Relaunched After Virus Found in Ghaziabad Sewage
2026-07-07.summary: An intensified vaccination drive was launched in Ghaziabad after a sewage sample from Vijay Nagar tested positive for vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV). Officials flagged 12 high-risk neighbourhoods (~1,50,000 residents); India was declared free of wild poliovirus in 2014.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Public-health surveillance, VDPV vs wild polio, and OPV/IPV are testable health-governance topics (GS 2), underlining why surveillance continues post-elimination.

deepDive.explanation: An intensified vaccination drive was launched in Ghaziabad (UP) after a sewage sample from Vijay Nagar (collected 5 June) tested positive for vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) — a rare strain that can circulate where immunisation coverage is low. Key facts: - Officials identified 12 neighbourhoods (~1,50,000 residents) as high-risk and intensified vaccination under the National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) and WHO watch - India was declared free of wild poliovirus in 2014 after decades of mass immunisation - Environmental surveillance (testing sewage for the virus) continues as an early-warning system even after wild-polio elimination Way forward: VDPV detection underlines why surveillance and high routine-immunisation coverage must continue post-elimination; any coverage gap can let the weakened vaccine virus regain virulence and circulate. Concepts: - Poliomyelitis — a viral disease causing paralysis, targeted for global eradication - Wild poliovirus (WPV) vs VDPV — WPV is the naturally circulating virus; VDPV arises when the weakened virus in Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV) mutates and spreads in under-vaccinated populations - OPV vs IPV — oral (live-attenuated) vs injectable (inactivated) polio vaccine — India uses both under the Pulse Polio Programme (from 1995)
prelimsFacts: India declared free of wild poliovirus in 2014
VDPV = mutated strain from the Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV)
Environmental surveillance = testing sewage for early warning
NCDC leads the response; WHO monitors
Pulse Polio Programme runs from 1995 (uses OPV + IPV)
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'Elimination is not eradication.' Using the VDPV detection in Ghaziabad, discuss the importance of disease surveillance and sustained immunisation in India's public-health system.

prelimsQs.q: India was certified free of wild poliovirus in which year?
prelimsQs.explanation: India was declared wild-polio-free in 2014, with the last case in 2011.

prelimsQs.q: 'Vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV)', which triggered the Ghaziabad response, arises from:
options: Mutation of the weakened strain in the Oral Polio Vaccine
A new wild strain from across the border
prelimsQs.explanation: VDPV is a mutated form of the live attenuated virus used in OPV, detectable via sewage surveillance.

2026-07-07.headline: NATO's Defence-Spending Push Strains Europe's Budgets
2026-07-07.summary: Ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, Secretary-General Mark Rutte will insist members are honouring pledges to raise defence spending to 5% of GDP by 2035 — but the Trump-driven push is stretching several European budgets, splitting members into fiscally comfortable and struggling camps.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: NATO burden-sharing, transatlantic strain and a multipolar security order shape the global environment for India's defence exports — relevant GS 2 IR.

deepDive.explanation: Ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, Secretary-General Mark Rutte will insist members are honouring pledges to raise defence spending — but the push, driven by President Trump, is stretching several European budgets. Key facts: - At last year's summit the 32-member alliance agreed to raise defence spending to 5% of GDP by 2035 — over double the 2025 European/Canada level - The 5% comprises 3.5% core military spending plus 1.5% on security-related items - European members plus Canada spent an extra $90 billion in real terms in 2025 vs 2024 - Two camps: Germany and Nordic/east-European states finding fiscal room, vs the UK, France and Italy struggling; the agenda also covers 'burden-shifting' from the US to Europe and scaling weapons production Way forward: The spending drive tests the fiscal limits of Europe's largest economies even as it aims to reduce dependence on the US — a recalibration relevant to India's reading of a multipolar security order and defence-export opportunities. Concepts: NATO — the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, a 32-member collective-defence alliance (Article 5: an attack on one is an attack on all). Defence-spending target — NATO's benchmark for members' military outlay (earlier 2%, now raised toward 5%). Burden-sharing/burden-shifting — the debate over Europe carrying a larger share of its own defence relative to the US.
prelimsFacts: NATO: 32 members; Article 5 collective defence
New target: 5% of GDP by 2035 (3.5% core + 1.5% security-related)
Earlier benchmark was 2% of GDP
NATO summit venue: Ankara, Turkey; SG = Mark Rutte
Europe+Canada spent $90bn more in 2025 vs 2024
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: NATO's shift toward a 5%-of-GDP defence target signals a recalibrating transatlantic order. Discuss its implications for global security and opportunities for India's defence sector.

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements about NATO: 1. It currently has 32 member countries. 2. Article 5 of its treaty embodies collective defence. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: NATO has 32 members after Finland and Sweden joined, and Article 5 is the collective-defence clause.

prelimsQs.q: NATO's newly adopted defence-spending goal for members by 2035 is:
prelimsQs.explanation: The 5%-by-2035 target splits into 3.5% core defence and 1.5% broader security spending, replacing the older 2% benchmark.

2026-07-07.headline: E-Rickshaw Battery 'Hacks' and Bluetooth BMS Vulnerabilities
2026-07-07.summary: Viral videos showed people wirelessly disabling e-rickshaw batteries over Bluetooth. The piece clarifies this is not Chinese 'sabotage' but exploitation of a long-known security flaw in imported Battery Management System (BMS) modules that lack authentication — pointing to a supply-chain security gap.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: IoT/BMS vulnerabilities and supply-chain security are emerging GS 3 cybersecurity themes with a concrete, memorable case study.

deepDive.explanation: Viral 'prank' videos showed individuals wirelessly disabling e-rickshaw batteries over Bluetooth using apps like BAT-BMS. The piece clarifies this is not high-tech Chinese 'sabotage' but the exploitation of a long-known security flaw in imported Battery Management System (BMS) modules. Key facts: - The apps connect to BMS modules made by a Chinese firm, Jiabaida; a GitHub discussion seven years ago flagged their lack of authentication - Almost all e-rickshaw components are imported from China (via Alibaba/AliExpress); only the frame/chassis is assembled domestically - The government asked Google and Apple to remove the battery apps — mirroring its blocking of Telegram during a re-NEET episode; the piece argues this treats symptoms, not the underlying vulnerability - Gazette notifications GSR 709(E) and S.O. 2590(E) (8.10.2014) set e-rickshaw norms (dimensions, brakes, tyres) but not electronic-component standards Way forward: Rather than banning apps, India needs supply-chain security regulation — vetting suppliers, auditing firmware, and tracing component origins — extended to all products with electronic components, so vulnerabilities are fixed before assembly. Concepts: A BMS monitors/protects a battery pack (voltage, temperature, charge); a hacked BMS can disable a vehicle. Authentication — verifying identity before granting control (absent here). Supply-chain security — managing risks from globally sourced components. IoT vulnerability — connected devices shipped with weak/no security.
prelimsFacts: BMS = Battery Management System (monitors voltage/temp/charge)
Flaw: imported modules (Jiabaida) lack authentication
Most e-rickshaw parts imported from China; only chassis local
2014 gazette norms cover dimensions/brakes, not electronics
Fix: supply-chain security (vet suppliers, audit firmware)
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'Banning apps treats the symptom, not the disease.' Discuss the supply-chain and IoT-security challenges exposed by e-rickshaw BMS vulnerabilities and the regulatory response needed.

prelimsQs.q: In electric vehicles, the 'BMS', recently in news over Bluetooth vulnerabilities, stands for:
prelimsQs.explanation: The BMS monitors cell voltage, temperature and charge; unauthenticated Bluetooth BMS modules were being switched off remotely.

prelimsQs.q: The e-rickshaw battery 'shutdown' videos pointed primarily to which underlying problem?
options: Absence of authentication in imported BMS modules
prelimsQs.explanation: The flaw is a known security gap — imported BMS modules lack authentication, a supply-chain security issue rather than sabotage.

2026-07-07.headline: UN's Guterres Warns AI Is Outpacing Oversight
2026-07-07.summary: At the first government-level UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance in Geneva, UN chief Guterres warned AI is developing faster than anyone can keep up, urging globally harmonised rules — especially to protect children. A UN panel found the US holds 75% of the world's top-500 AI supercomputing power.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Global AI governance, compute concentration and the digital divide are fast-rising GS 2/GS 3 topics; the UN Global Dialogue is a fresh institutional development.

deepDive.explanation: UN Secretary-General António Guterres warned that AI is developing faster than anyone can keep up, urging globally harmonised rules — especially to protect children — at the first-ever government-level UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance in Geneva (6–7 July). Key facts: - 'Innovation needs guardrails… If AI is to be powerful, it must be governed,' he told delegates; the two-day dialogue is not a treaty but a rule-setting discussion - Delegates will consider a report by a UN-backed independent panel of 40 experts — the first global scientific assessment of AI - The report found AI power is highly concentrated: the US holds 75% of the computing power among the world's top 500 AI supercomputers, China 15% - Guterres proposed an 'AI Child Safety Pledge' requiring firms to prove systems are safe for children, and noted the internet took 15 years to reach a billion people while AI took two Way forward: Guterres framed AI as a potential 'great equalizer' if governed well, but warned developing countries risk being left behind by concentrated compute — reinforcing calls for inclusive, Global-South-sensitive global AI rules. Concepts: The UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance is a new UN process mandated by the Global Digital Compact (2024), alongside an International Scientific Panel on AI. Compute concentration — dominance of AI computing capacity by a few firms/states, a driver of the digital divide.
prelimsFacts: UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance: Geneva, 6-7 July 2026
Mandated by the Global Digital Compact (2024)
US holds 75% of top-500 AI supercomputing power; China 15%
UN panel of 40 experts = first global scientific assessment of AI
Guterres proposed an 'AI Child Safety Pledge'

prelimsQs.explanation: The Dialogue, mandated by the Global Digital Compact (2024), met in Geneva on 6-7 July 2026.

prelimsQs.explanation: The US holds ~75% and China ~15% of top-500 AI supercomputing capacity.

2026-07-07.headline: China Test-Fires 'Strategic' Missile into the Pacific
2026-07-07.summary: A Chinese nuclear submarine test-fired a 'strategic' missile with a dummy warhead into the Pacific, drawing condemnation from regional states. The launch — timed with an Australia-Fiji defence treaty and China-Russia naval drills — signals intensifying great-power competition in the South Pacific.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: China's strategic signalling, the nuclear triad and South Pacific contestation directly shape India's Indo-Pacific stakes — a strong GS 2 IR/security item.

deepDive.explanation: A Chinese nuclear submarine test-fired a 'strategic' missile carrying a dummy (training-simulation) warhead into the Pacific Ocean on Monday, drawing condemnation from regional states. New Zealand described it as a nuclear-capable 'long-range ballistic missile,' though Beijing did not confirm whether an ICBM was used. Key facts: - The missile 'accurately landed in the designated sea area' at 12:01 pm (04:01 GMT) - It followed China's 2023 firing of an ICBM near French Polynesia — its first such launch over international waters in 40+ years - New Zealand (FM Winston Peters) called the Pacific 'an Ocean of Peace'; Australia called the test 'destabilising,' Japan voiced 'serious concerns,' Taiwan said China was 'demonstrating its military might' - Occurred the same day Australia and Fiji signed a major defence treaty, and as China began annual joint naval exercises with Russia off Qingdao - Russia defended the test as China's 'sovereign right' Way forward: The launch — timed with the Australia-Fiji pact and China-Russia drills — signals intensifying great-power competition in the South Pacific, reinforcing India's stakes in a stable, rules-based Indo-Pacific. Concepts: ICBM — range over 5,500 km, typically nuclear-capable. SLBM — Submarine-Launched Ballistic Missile, giving a survivable 'second-strike' capability. PLA Rocket Force — the branch controlling China's missiles. Strategic (nuclear) triad — delivery by land (ICBMs), sea (SLBMs) and air (bombers).
prelimsFacts: ICBM: range over 5,500 km, typically nuclear-capable
SLBM: submarine-launched, enables 'second-strike'
China's 2023 ICBM test was its first over intl waters in 40+ years
PLA Rocket Force controls China's missiles
Nuclear triad = land (ICBM) + sea (SLBM) + air (bombers)

prelimsQs.explanation: ICBMs have ranges above 5,500 km and are typically nuclear-capable.

options: Army, Navy and Air Force commands
Land-based missiles, submarine-launched missiles and strategic bombers
prelimsQs.explanation: The triad = land-based ICBMs + sea-based SLBMs + air-delivered weapons, ensuring second-strike capability.

2026-07-07.headline: 'Prioritise Charging Infrastructure for Electric Three-Wheelers' — MoSPI
2026-07-07.summary: A MoSPI report says commercial three-wheeler charging corridors should be prioritised, as e-3Ws account for 61% of electricity demand from India's EV fleet despite two-wheelers dominating fleet numbers. Total EV electricity demand is ~20 TWh/year, projected to rise sharply by 2030.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: EV energy transition, grid planning and charging/battery-swapping infrastructure are practical GS 3 Economy/Environment themes backed by fresh official data.

deepDive.explanation: A Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI) report says commercial three-wheeler charging corridors should be prioritised, as e-3Ws account for the majority of electricity demand from India's EV fleet. Key facts (India EV Electricity Demand Assessment Report, by the Expert Committee on Energy Statistics): - Electric three-wheelers make up 61% of annual electricity demand from India's EV population (~8.97 crore vehicles) - 2-wheelers dominate fleet numbers but draw less power (lower distance and per-km consumption) than 3Ws and heavy vehicles - Total EV electricity demand is 20.07 TWh/year — ~1.1% of India's generation; NITI Aayog projects it may rise to 100–640 TWh/year by 2030 - Category-wise quantification is needed for future grid/charging planning Way forward: Targeting charging (and battery-swapping) infrastructure at the highest-utilisation segment — commercial e-3Ws — offers the biggest returns for grid planning and EV adoption, informing schemes like FAME/PM E-DRIVE. Concepts: MoSPI — the ministry for India's official statistics (NSO, CSO). EV charging corridor — a network of charging stations along high-traffic routes. Battery swapping — exchanging a depleted battery for a charged one, especially suited to 2W/3W commercial fleets. NITI Aayog — the government's policy think tank modelling EV-adoption scenarios.
prelimsFacts: e-3Ws = 61% of EV electricity demand (though 2Ws dominate numbers)
Total EV electricity demand: 20.07 TWh/year (~1.1% of generation)
NITI Aayog projects 100-640 TWh/year by 2030
Report by MoSPI's Expert Committee on Energy Statistics
Battery swapping best suited to 2W/3W fleets

prelimsQs.explanation: Commercial e-3Ws dominate electricity draw due to heavy daily usage, though 2Ws dominate fleet numbers.

prelimsQs.explanation: Small standardised batteries make swapping practical for 2W/3W fleets.

2026-07-07.headline: White Gut Disease Outbreak in Andhra Pradesh Shrimp Ponds
2026-07-07.summary: Andhra Pradesh's Fisheries Department flagged an outbreak of White Gut Disease (White Feces Disease) in shrimp ponds in West Godavari, urging preventive measures. AP is India's largest shrimp-producing State, and the export-oriented industry is a major forex earner.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Aquaculture, the blue economy and export-oriented shrimp farming are niche but scoring GS 3 agriculture topics; biosecurity is the key policy angle.

deepDive.explanation: The Fisheries Department of Andhra Pradesh sounded an alarm over an outbreak of White Gut Disease (WGD) — also called White Feces Disease (WFD) — in shrimp ponds, appealing to farmers to take preventive measures. AP is India's largest shrimp-producing State. Key facts: - Symptoms confirmed in ponds in West Godavari district (Undi mandal); samples of shrimp, water, faeces and feed sent to labs at Kaikalur and Bhimavaram - Affected shrimp take in less feed, suffer stunted growth, have brown intestines and loose shells - Farmers advised to maintain good pond hygiene; a task force is collecting samples statewide Way forward: Early detection, biosecurity and hygiene are critical to protect the export-oriented shrimp industry (a major forex earner) from disease-driven losses; sustained surveillance and farmer awareness are key to the Blue Economy and doubling of aquaculture incomes. Concepts: - White Gut/White Feces Disease — a shrimp disease (linked to Vibrio bacteria, the microsporidian EHP, and poor pond conditions) marked by white gut/faeces, reduced feeding and stunted growth - Aquaculture — farming of aquatic organisms; India is the second-largest fish producer and a top shrimp exporter (mainly L. vannamei) - Relevant bodies: CIBA (Central Institute of Brackishwater Aquaculture) and MPEDA (Marine Products Export Development Authority)
prelimsFacts: AP = India's largest shrimp-producing State
WGD linked to Vibrio bacteria, EHP microsporidian, poor ponds
India = 2nd-largest fish producer; top shrimp exporter (L. vannamei)
CIBA = Central Institute of Brackishwater Aquaculture
MPEDA = Marine Products Export Development Authority

prelimsQs.q: India's largest shrimp-producing State is:
prelimsQs.explanation: Andhra Pradesh leads Indian shrimp production, centred on its coastal aquaculture belt.

prelimsQs.q: MPEDA, associated with India's seafood sector, is the:
prelimsQs.explanation: MPEDA under the Commerce Ministry promotes marine-product exports.

2026-07-07.headline: Tibetan Community Marks Dalai Lama's 91st Birthday
2026-07-07.summary: The 91st birthday of the 14th Dalai Lama was celebrated by the Tibetan community across Himachal Pradesh, with the principal ceremony at Shewatsel Teaching Ground in Leh, Ladakh, where he is visiting. His reincarnation/succession remains a live India-China geopolitical issue.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: The Dalai Lama, the CTA and the succession question sit at the intersection of culture, diaspora and India-China relations — useful for GS 1 and GS 2.

deepDive.explanation: The 91st birthday of the 14th Dalai Lama was celebrated by the Tibetan community across Himachal Pradesh, with the principal ceremony at the Shewatsel Teaching Ground in Leh, Ladakh, where he is currently visiting. Key facts: - The Dalai Lama was born on 6 July 1935 in Taktser village, Amdo, Tibet - HP CM Sukhvinder Singh Sukhu called him 'a global symbol of compassion, non-violence and universal human values' - Celebrations also took place at the Tsuglagkhang Monastery in McLeodganj (Dharamshala, Kangra) — headquarters of the Central Tibetan Administration (CTA) Concepts: - The Dalai Lama is the highest spiritual leader of Tibetan Buddhism (Gelug school); the 14th, Tenzin Gyatso, has lived in exile in India since 1959 - The Central Tibetan Administration (CTA) is the Tibetan government-in-exile based in Dharamshala (not officially recognised by any state) - Reincarnation/succession — the process of identifying the next Dalai Lama is a live geopolitical issue, as China claims a say, which the Dalai Lama and CTA reject

Born 6 July 1935 in Taktser, Amdo, Tibet
CTA (government-in-exile) is based in Dharamshala (McLeodganj)
Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism
Succession disputed — China claims a role, CTA rejects it

prelimsQs.explanation: The CTA is based at McLeodganj, Dharamshala, Himachal Pradesh.

2026-07-07.headline: Rare Bengal Tiger Cubs Born at Cuba's National Zoo
2026-07-07.summary: The birth of four Bengal tiger cubs, including a rare white tiger, at the Cuban National Zoo in Havana is being celebrated as a 'small miracle'. The Bengal tiger is native to the Indian subcontinent — India holds ~70% of the world's wild tigers — and the white tiger is a leucistic colour variant, not a species.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Tiger conservation, ex-situ vs in-situ conservation and leucism vs albinism are clean GS 3 environment facts often asked in Prelims.

deepDive.explanation: The birth of four Bengal tiger cubs, including a rare white tiger, at the Cuban National Zoo in Havana is being celebrated as a 'small miracle' on an island facing shortages of fuel, medicine and power — only the second time white tigers have been born in Cuba. Key facts: - The Bengal tiger (Panthera tigris tigris) is native to the Indian subcontinent; India holds ~70% of the world's wild tigers - The white tiger is not a separate species/subspecies but a leucistic colour variant caused by a recessive gene - Such captive breeding is ex-situ conservation (outside natural habitat) Concepts: - Ex-situ vs in-situ conservation — breeding/protecting species outside their habitat (zoos, gene banks) vs within it (tiger reserves, national parks) - Leucism — partial loss of pigmentation (white coat), distinct from albinism (which also affects eye colour) - The Bengal tiger is IUCN 'Endangered'; India's national animal, conserved under Project Tiger (1973) and the NTCA
prelimsFacts: Bengal tiger = Panthera tigris tigris; India has ~70% of wild tigers
White tiger = leucistic variant (recessive gene), not a separate species
Leucism ≠ albinism (albinism also affects eye colour)
IUCN status: Endangered
Conserved under Project Tiger (1973) & the NTCA
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: Distinguish between ex-situ and in-situ conservation. Evaluate their relative roles in tiger conservation, using India's Project Tiger as a reference.

prelimsQs.q: Consider the following statements about the white tiger: 1. It is a separate subspecies of tiger. 2. Its colouration results from leucism, a recessive genetic variant. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsQs.explanation: The white tiger is merely a leucistic colour variant of the Bengal tiger, not a species or subspecies.

prelimsQs.q: India holds approximately what share of the world's wild tiger population?
prelimsQs.explanation: India harbours ~70% of wild tigers, conserved under Project Tiger (1973) and the NTCA.

2026-07-07.headline: Bihar Launches Heli-Tourism and Air-Tourism Scheme
2026-07-07.summary: The Bihar Cabinet approved the CM Heli-Tourism and Air Tourism Scheme, 2026, launching subsidised helicopter and fixed-wing services from 15 July to connect Patna with Rajgir, Kaimur and Valmikinagar, plus weekend joyrides over Patna. It also cleared a Sindhu Darshan pilgrimage grant.
2026-07-07.whyItMatters: Tourism as regional development and connectivity is a GS 3 economy/GS 2 governance theme; the scheme names are Prelims-worthy state-scheme facts.

deepDive.explanation: The Bihar Cabinet approved the Chief Minister Heli-Tourism and Air Tourism Scheme, 2026, launching subsidised helicopter and fixed-wing services from 15 July (six-month pilot) to connect Patna with Rajgir, Kaimur and Valmikinagar, plus weekend joyrides over Patna at Rs 2,100. Key facts: - Aims to cut travel time to key heritage, religious and wildlife destinations and expand tourism as an economic activity - The Cabinet also approved the Sindhu Darshan Pilgrimage Financial Assistance Grant Scheme — reimbursement up to Rs 20,000 for Bihar residents visiting the Indus (Sindhu) river at Leh-Ladakh, for up to 100 beneficiaries/year - Comes alongside revenue-raising steps like tolls on state highways Concepts: - Heli-tourism — use of helicopter services to improve access to remote tourist/pilgrimage sites, common in hilly and heritage circuits - Rajgir/Valmikinagar — major Bihar tourism nodes: Rajgir (Buddhist/Jain heritage, Nalanda circuit) and Valmikinagar (tiger reserve on the Indo-Nepal border) - Sindhu Darshan — an annual festival at the Indus river in Ladakh promoting national integration and tourism
prelimsFacts: CM Heli-Tourism & Air Tourism Scheme, 2026 (Bihar); pilot from 15 July
Connects Patna with Rajgir, Kaimur, Valmikinagar
Sindhu Darshan grant: up to Rs 20,000, 100 beneficiaries/year
Rajgir = Buddhist/Jain heritage (Nalanda circuit)
Valmikinagar = tiger reserve on the Indo-Nepal border
deepDive.possibleMainsQuestion: 'Tourism can be a powerful engine of regional development.' Examine how connectivity schemes like heli-tourism can unlock the economic potential of heritage and wildlife destinations.

prelimsQs.q: Valmikinagar, connected under Bihar's new heli-tourism scheme, is known for:
options: A tiger reserve on the Indo-Nepal border
A hydropower dam on the Ganga
prelimsQs.explanation: Valmiki Tiger Reserve lies in West Champaran on the Indo-Nepal border.

prelimsQs.q: Rajgir, part of the Nalanda heritage circuit, is associated with which religions' heritage?
prelimsQs.explanation: Rajgir carries major Buddhist and Jain heritage sites near ancient Nalanda.
